structured.summary: This paper investigates methods to approximate human similarity judgments across images, audio, and video modalities. It evaluates over 600 pre-trained deep neural networks (DNNs), proposes novel language-based approaches using captions and adaptive tag collection (STEP-Tag), and introduces stacked methods combining language and sensory embeddings to improve approximation accuracy while reducing human annotation costs.

strengths.point: Comprehensive large-scale evaluation of 611 pre-trained models across three modalities.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.1 and Figure 3 detail the extensive benchmarking of DNN models for images, audio, and video.

strengths.point: Introduction of a novel adaptive tagging pipeline (STEP-Tag) for efficient and high-quality tag collection.
strengths.evidence: Section 2.2.4 describes STEP-Tag and Supplementary Section B.6 validates its effectiveness compared to baselines.

strengths.point: Demonstration that language-based methods are more cost-effective and outperform DNN-only methods in approximating human similarity.
strengths.evidence: Abstract and Section 4 show language-based methods require O(N) human judgments and improve performance over DNN-based methods.

strengths.point: Development of stacked methods combining language embeddings with DNN embeddings that consistently yield the best approximations.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.3 and results in Section 4 highlight that stacked embeddings outperform individual modalities across all tested datasets.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of how different aggregation methods for multiple captions affect similarity approximation.
weaknesses.evidence: Section C.1 mentions averaging caption embeddings and a brief test of concatenation but does not extensively explore alternative aggregation strategies.

weaknesses.point: Potential bias and societal implications of language-based similarity approximations are acknowledged but not deeply analyzed.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5 discusses risks of embedding human biases but lacks concrete mitigation strategies or empirical analysis.

weaknesses.point: The study focuses on English-language captions and tags, limiting immediate applicability to low-resource or non-English languages.
weaknesses.evidence: While the paper mentions potential for low-resource languages, all data collection and models are English-based as described in Sections 2.2 and 3.2.

weaknesses.point: The performance gap between stacked methods and individual modalities, though consistent, is modest, raising questions about practical gains.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4 notes the modest size of the performance gap between stacked and single-modality methods.

weaknesses.point: The adaptive tag collection method STEP-Tag requires multiple iterations and participant interactions, which may still be costly or time-consuming.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2.4 and Supplementary B.6 describe STEP-Tag involving up to 20 iterations per stimulus.

weaknesses.point: Limited analysis of failure cases or domains where language-based methods might underperform compared to DNNs or classical methods.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not provide detailed error analysis or domain-specific limitations beyond general discussion.

weaknesses.point: The reliance on pre-trained large language models may limit applicability in domains with no suitable LLMs available.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.2.1 acknowledges that in some niche domains or low-resource languages, LLMs may be unavailable or out-of-distribution.

questions.question: How sensitive are the language-based similarity approximations to the quality and diversity of the collected captions and tags?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify the robustness of the approach and guide data collection efforts.

questions.question: Can the STEP-Tag pipeline be adapted or optimized to reduce the number of iterations or participant burden while maintaining tag quality?
questions.motivation: Reducing annotation cost and time is critical for scalability and practical deployment.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or tested cross-lingual or multilingual extensions of their language-based methods for low-resource languages?
questions.motivation: This would demonstrate the generalizability and applicability of the approach beyond English.

questions.question: What are the implications of the modest performance gains from stacking embeddings for real-world applications? Are the additional costs justified?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this would help practitioners decide whether to adopt stacked methods.

critique_points.point_001: Limited exploration of how different aggregation methods for multiple captions affect similarity approximation. (Section C.1 mentions averaging caption embeddings and a brief test of concatenation but does not extensively explore alternative aggregation strategies.)
critique_points.point_002: Potential bias and societal implications of language-based similarity approximations are acknowledged but not deeply analyzed. (Section 5 discusses risks of embedding human biases but lacks concrete mitigation strategies or empirical analysis.)
critique_points.point_003: The study focuses on English-language captions and tags, limiting immediate applicability to low-resource or non-English languages. (While the paper mentions potential for low-resource languages, all data collection and models are English-based as described in Sections 2.2 and 3.2.)
critique_points.point_004: The performance gap between stacked methods and individual modalities, though consistent, is modest, raising questions about practical gains. (Section 4 notes the modest size of the performance gap between stacked and single-modality methods.)
critique_points.point_005: The adaptive tag collection method STEP-Tag requires multiple iterations and participant interactions, which may still be costly or time-consuming. (Section 2.2.4 and Supplementary B.6 describe STEP-Tag involving up to 20 iterations per stimulus.)
critique_points.point_006: Limited analysis of failure cases or domains where language-based methods might underperform compared to DNNs or classical methods. (The paper does not provide detailed error analysis or domain-specific limitations beyond general discussion.)
critique_points.point_007: The reliance on pre-trained large language models may limit applicability in domains with no suitable LLMs available. (Section 3.2.1 acknowledges that in some niche domains or low-resource languages, LLMs may be unavailable or out-of-distribution.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive are the language-based similarity approximations to the quality and diversity of the collected captions and tags? (Understanding this would clarify the robustness of the approach and guide data collection efforts.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the STEP-Tag pipeline be adapted or optimized to reduce the number of iterations or participant burden while maintaining tag quality? (Reducing annotation cost and time is critical for scalability and practical deployment.)
critique_points.point_010: Have the authors considered or tested cross-lingual or multilingual extensions of their language-based methods for low-resource languages? (This would demonstrate the generalizability and applicability of the approach beyond English.)
critique_points.point_011: What are the implications of the modest performance gains from stacking embeddings for real-world applications? Are the additional costs justified? (Clarifying this would help practitioners decide whether to adopt stacked methods.)
